era_context: The Middle Bronze Age in the Levant and Egypt; the Middle Kingdom in Egypt under the 12th Dynasty (Amenemhat III, late reign or successor). Babylon under Hammurabi's successors in the Old Babylonian period. The Indus Valley Civilization at its mature phase. Mycenaean Greece in its early stages. This is a period of established state systems, long-distance trade networks, and codified law.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; archaeological evidence sparse for this specific year)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies; no year-specific events recoverable)
Oceania (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise)
Americas (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise for this period)

events.title: Reign of Amenemhat III continues or transitions to successor in Egypt
events.description: Amenemhat III of the 12th Dynasty was one of Egypt's longest-reigning and most prosperous pharaohs. By 1911 BCE, he was in his final years or had recently died (dating uncertain; reign estimates range c. 1860–1814 BCE). His reign saw extensive building projects, including the Labyrinth at Hawara and the completion of irrigation infrastructure. The transition to his successor (likely Amenemhat IV) marks a period of continued stability but gradual decline in centralized power that would lead to the Second Intermediate Period.
key_figures: Amenemhat IV (successor, if not yet reigning)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hawara and Lahun (excavations by Flinders Petrie and others)

events.certainty_note: Absolute dating of the 12th Dynasty is debated. Conventional chronology places Amenemhat III c. 1860–1814 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates up to a century earlier or later. The year 1911 BCE is assigned by convention within the conventional chronology but carries ±20–30 year uncertainty.

events.title: Hammurabi's successors consolidate Babylonian power in Mesopotamia
events.description: Hammurabi died c. 1750 BCE (conventional chronology); by 1911 BCE, his successors (likely Samsu-iluna or a later Old Babylonian king) were ruling. The Old Babylonian state was consolidating territorial control and managing the complex network of vassal states and rival powers. This period saw the maintenance of the Code of Hammurabi as a legal framework and continued cuneiform administration.

sources.name: Babylonian King List A and B (cuneiform tablets)
Cuneiform administrative texts and contracts from Babylon

events.certainty_note: The Old Babylonian chronology is contested. The 'conventional' or 'middle' chronology places Hammurabi c. 1792–1750 BCE; the 'low' chronology places him c. 1696–1654 BCE. This affects the identity of the reigning king in 1911 BCE by up to 100 years. Assignment here is speculative.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the 12th Dynasty and Old Babylonian period is one of the most contentious issues in ancient Near Eastern chronology. The 'conventional' chronology used here is increasingly challenged by scholars who favor a 'low' or 'ultra-low' chronology, which would shift these events by 50–150 years. Additionally, the significance of individual regnal years in this period is difficult to assess without contemporary records; most 'events' are reconstructions from later sources or archaeological stratigraphy, which do not preserve year-specific information. No disconfirming evidence specific to 1911 BCE has been identified, but the entire chronological framework is provisional.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1911 BCE is heavily dependent on later compilations (King Lists, Manetho) and archaeological stratigraphy, which cannot reliably pinpoint individual years. Contemporary written records (cuneiform tablets, Egyptian administrative texts) exist but are rarely dated to a specific regnal year in a way that allows modern scholars to identify events in 1911 BCE with confidence. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and religious activities; economic, social, and demographic changes are largely invisible. Chronological uncertainty of ±30–50 years is standard for this period.

graph_edges.note: The decline of centralized power under Amenemhat III's successors contributed to the fragmentation that characterized the Second Intermediate Period.
